text: This is Dr. Ben Taylor, cardiothoracic surgeon at St. Alphonsus in Boise.
I'll be showing you a video today of a left upper lobectomy done with the da Vinci robot system.
You're gonna see why, if you have lung cancer, you're gonna wanna have your surgery here with us at St. Alphonsus in Boise.
We are the only hospital system in Idaho performing robotic lobectomies.
The video will show you why this is the state of the art way to treat lung cancer surgically.
Lung cancer surgery used to be done in an open fashion with a very large incision and what we would consider today to be fairly crude instruments.
Now the robot allows the surgeon to have three arms, advanced energy for dissection.
It allows us to operate in very small spaces with very small incisions.
Basically to remove a lobe of the lung, you have to divide all the arteries to that lobe, all the veins to that lobe, and the airways to that lobe.
The robot is perfect for this application.
You can see with the stapler device, the instruments with their tremendous range of movement and dexterity really allows us to perform
The video shows the heart inside the pericardium on the left side of the surgical field, with small incisions visible as part of the elegant surgical approach.
The surgeon uses a robotic instrument to divide the pulmonary vein leading to the upper lobe of the lung, demonstrating precise control in a confined surgical space.
The robotic stapler is used to seal and divide a vessel, demonstrating its dual function in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to address the airway to the upper lobe, focusing on the bronchus as the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses the robotic stapler to safely seal and divide the airway to the upper lobe, demonstrating the precision and dexterity of the instrument.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide the bronchus, completing the separation of the airway to the upper lobe.
Usually the last step in an operation of this sort.
The surgeon places the excised thymus specimen into a specialized retrieval bag, completing the thymectomy procedure.
